file: How to run a Post-Mortem meeting, step-by-step
brief: Such an unsafe environment  actively prevents people from learning from their mistakes. There is something in the team or the process that made this person press this button believing it would not  bring the system down. Blame prevents people from  speaking up with concerns, from pointing out each other's mistakes and blind spots. Psychological safety is the belief that you won't be punished or humiliated for speaking up with ideas, questions, concerns or mistakes.
sourceTitle: How to run a Post-Mortem meeting, step-by-step
license: Public YouTube video processed transiently. Bonfyre retained only derived artifacts.
sourceCopy: Source: Bran van der Meer on YouTube. Bonfyre downloaded, transcribed, and deleted the media. Only the transcript, brief, and proof remain.
whyItMatters: Incident reviews become a searchable knowledge base — patterns emerge across postmortems that prevent repeated failures.
searchIntro: Search across the postmortem atlas records to see how Bonfyre connects related content from different sources.

brief: You have had a war, you spent hours, multiple  people, right, multiple teams, all of you screaming, shouting, yelling, hopefully not, hopefully it's  more civilized and professional, right, and you go through your job and come up from an incident. Many different things could have happened,  don't forget any of the details, timeline, an incident taking an X amount of time to get  resolved is an important benchmark, right. You go back and understand what are the key takeaways from that,  understand what worked, what did not work, right. What does that mean to you in terms of business, in  terms of loss of usage, right, loss of revenue, use of goodwill, right.
